nativeRange.habitat: Clear lagoon and seaward reefs; adults typically observed at 1–8 m, with the species recorded between roughly 2–100 m. Juveniles tend to stay solitary inside caves and under ledges.

diet.notes: Feeds primarily on benthic hard-shelled invertebrates such as molluscs, small crustaceans, and worms. In captivity has been observed to accept a varied carnivore diet of vitamin-enriched frozen mysis and brine shrimp, krill, finely chopped clam or shrimp, and quality marine flakes/pellets. Juveniles have been documented picking parasites from larger fishes in the wild.

summary: A mid-sized Indo-Pacific wrasse reaching about 20 cm, with a striking black-and-white juvenile pattern that fades to a reddish-bodied adult. Generally considered reef-safe with caution and best in a large fish-tolerant system.
careNotes:
The Axilspot Hogfish is generally considered an intermediate-level marine wrasse. Juveniles are striking — black with rows of white spots — and have been documented acting as cleaners, picking at parasites on larger fishes. Adults develop a reddish anterior and white posterior with a distinctive dark blotch at the pectoral-fin base (the 'axil spot'), and tend to be more assertive than the juveniles. Sources describe temperament inconsistently, ranging from 'peaceful' (LiveAquaria) to 'semi-aggressive' or 'aggressive' (Reef2Reef, Reefapp); a semi-aggressive baseline is the safest assumption, particularly toward conspecifics, similarly-shaped wrasses, and very docile tankmates.

Reef compatibility is best described as 'with caution'. Adults have been known to prey on small ornamental shrimp, small crabs, snails, tubeworms, and small bivalves; corals are not typically targeted. If kept with a reef, introduce the hogfish last and avoid stocking small motile invertebrates afterward.

Tank-size recommendations across sources spread widely — LiveAquaria lists a 70-gallon (~265 L) minimum, Reefapp suggests roughly 400 L, and Reef2Reef recommends 125 gallons (~473 L). For an active 20-cm wrasse that 'revels in swimming' and benefits from a long open footprint, a 400 L tank with a length of roughly 150 cm is a sensible minimum, and going larger is generally preferable for full-grown adults.

Like most wrasses, this species is a protogynous (female-first) sequential hermaphrodite. Hogfishes are also commonly reported jumpers, so a well-fitted lid is generally advised.

Cross-source agreement was good on size (20 cm), water temperature (mostly 24–27 °C), and reef caution, but tank-size and temperament showed real spread. Values written here reflect averaged, cautious choices rather than any single source.
breedingNotes: Reported to be oviparous with distinct pair formation during spawning. As a protogynous hermaphrodite, individuals typically begin life as females, with dominant individuals transitioning to male. Captive breeding has not been documented in home aquariums.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Species detail page lists: 8 in adult, 70 gal minimum, 72–78 °F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12, peaceful (slightly territorial with conspecifics), reef-safe with caution, carnivore, moderate care level, Indo-Pacific.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked max body length (20 cm SL), taxonomy (Labridae, Eupercaria), Indo-Pacific distribution (Red Sea to French Polynesia), depth range (2–100 m), preferred temperature (24.4–28.9 °C, mean 27.5 °C), diet (hard-shelled invertebrates), juvenile cleaning behaviour, oviparous spawning with distinct pairing, and IUCN status (Least Concern, 2008).

additional.name: Reefapp
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (20 cm), minimum tank size (~400 L), temperature (~26 °C), salinity (~1.026), and reef-with-caution rating. Notes the species 'revels in swimming' — used to bias the chosen tank volume toward the larger end of the cross-source spread.

additional.name: Reef2Reef
additional.notes: Used as an additional husbandry opinion via web-search summary. Suggests a 125 gal (~473 L) minimum, semi-aggressive temperament, and known jumper (justifies tight lid).

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented. Wild spawning is oviparous with distinct pair formation; the species is a protogynous sequential hermaphrodite.
